comments.author: @Wolfpeltdrifter
comments.text: Estonia looks like a paradise except for their digital id

comments.author: @stenliikane5753
comments.text:
1. Mobile internet in deep forest is so-so. In some places the signal may be good/adequate but in some areas where you are not in deep forest but in a village which is surrounded by forest the signal may be zero. It depends highly from a specific location, where the nearest TV masts are and so on. 
2. Story about the general  "morning routine" is a fairy tale- it depends from person to a person.
3. Culture with no honking horns- this is a joke, Tallinn's traffic is super aggressive, everyone are seemingly very busy and the blood is boiling even in Saturday 1 PM while turning the wheel. On the contrary you get a honking horn from every car when you don't speed up in 2 seconds while sitting behind the red light and now it has turned green. 
4. We do have some electric buses in Tallinn at least, but not many.
5. Estonia is very digital society yes but the other side of the coin is that it gives an opportunity for companies to shut down physical offices (for example bank offices, post offices, various network providers and so on). The problem is that people 0-50 are digital but older people in many cases don't even know how to use the computer, let alone paying invoices in the internet for example. 
6. Some shots in the video are not from Estonia- 6.51 is either in Latvia or Lithuania I think.  Also showing some Cossack performance is very far from Estonian culture.  
7. During St. John's day indeed the towns seem definitely more empty (only Russians are working as it is nothing) but singing and dancing is not a thing. Simply being together and so on- and yes bonfire is a must. Although Estonians 150-200 years ago had more special days during the year when the bonfire was a must, then St. John's day bonfire has preserved. During St. George's day and May Day for example old Estonians made a bonfire too.
8. Remote work is definitely not very widespread and common although it gained popularity during COVID. It depends from the company. 
9. Hierarchy is on the contrary quite important in companies. They tend to be less important in new/innovative companies with younger and more loose leaders. Yea well software developer- c'mon.... not everyone are software developers. 
10. Drones in forestry- not very widespread although there is somewhat usage
11. Potato yes is one of the pillars of the cuisine although it has been as an ingridient only from early 18th century. Estonians started to use potatos as a food during the Great Famine of 1695-97 which rampaged in Finland, Latvia and Sweden too. Before that potato were used only as animal food.
12. Shared family meals are definitely not common. Some do- some not.  
13. Real time info about the public transport coming in to a bus/tram stop are definitely not everywhere in Tallinn. 
14. Technology in Estonian educations system. Yes and no- the schools are very different (especially further from bigger settlements). World class education (meaning innovative teachers with state of art teaching methods) is true to an extent. Older teachers are definitely not so innovative and not very tech savvy.  Thing like E-School is nation wide yes and every school is linked with it- students see their grades, homework and so on from the portal.  
15. Coding in primary school is not very common though. It very much depends from a school. 
16. System of learning languages in the schools is nation wide. Foreign language A (English) starts when students are 8-9. Foreign language B (usually Russian or German) starts when students are starting the MIddle School. Foreign language C (French, Spanish etc.)  when students start his/her Gymansium/High School. Some schools are teaching Finnish, Greek, Italian or even Hebrew and Latin but it all depends if the school will find a teacher to teach the specific language. 
17.Studying abroad is so-so, some do- some not. Estonians coming back from abroad is also debatable- there is not a very big flow to out or in. 
18. Estonians will return to raise kids/ start a family not to start a company in Estonia. 
19. 27.21 the word about November is not a word for November as such, the narrator doesen't say that the name it says is taken from the Estonian folk calendar. In the folk calendar November was called as Hingedekuu (The month of the souls). Linked with All Souls' Day.
